title: podman machine set --disk-size doesn't seem to reflect on containers
body:
### Issue Description

I tried to increase the disk size of my containers running `podman machine set --disk-size` but even tho `podman machine inspect` shows the new value any new or existing container will still report the old disk size

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. `podman machine stop`
2. `podman machine set --disk-size <new value>`
3. `podman machine start`
4. `podman exec -it <some new or existing container> df -h`



### Describe the results you received

The container will still report the old disk size 

### Describe the results you expected

The container should report the new disk size

comments.body:
Yes, basically the operating system doesn't automatically detect the new sizing here.

EDIT: Actually, I don't think `set --disk-size` ends up doing anything right now.  IIRC I had to also do e.g. `truncate -s 300G ~/.local/share/containers/podman/machine/applehv/podman-machine-default-arm64.raw`

Arguably, the podman-machine code should do this; increase size, but don't allow decreasing as that would corrupt the disk.

And then after you've booted, run these steps inside `podman machine ssh` (as root)

- `/sbin/growpart /dev/vda 4`
- `unshare -m /bin/sh -c 'mount -o remount,rw /sysroot && xfs_growfs /sysroot'`
(Arguably of course...we should do this by default in the OS...wouldn't be hard)

I agree, especially when this feature is available via the GUI, it doesn't make much sense from a UE perspective to have this configurable but requires terminal (SSH) access to the Podman VM to have it actually reflect in the GUI. Thanks for the fix above @cgwalters++

this is still a thing. even a year later. I can increase the size although it is not reflected in the containers. it still shows the old size. And I have only two containers running. 
and trying the fix above by @cgwalters did nothing for me. 

```
➜  podman exec -it conatiner-1 df -h
Filesystem                            Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
overlay                                13G  9.4G  2.8G  78% /
tmpfs                                  64M     0   64M   0% /dev
tmpfs                                 910M  724K  909M   1% /etc/hosts
shm                                    63M  4.0K   63M   1% /dev/shm
a2a0ee2c717462feb1de2f5afd59de5fd2d8  229G  181G   48G  80% /app/backend/data
devtmpfs                              4.0M     0  4.0M   0% /proc/keys
```

➜  podman machine inspect

               },

          },
          "Created": "2025-01-29T09:28:10.528768+01:00",

          "Resources": {

          },

          "Rootful": true,
          "Rosetta": true
     }

Ran into this as well. Here is the list of steps that worked:

1. Resize using the UI or `--set-disk` or `qemu-img resize ~/.local/share/containers/podman/machine/qemu/podman-machine-default_fedora-coreos-*.qcow2 250G` (ensure that's the right location of your disk image)
2. Start the machine  `podman machine start`
3. Grow the partition  `podman machine ssh -- "growpart /dev/vda 4"`
5. Expand the filesystem  `podman machine ssh -- "sudo xfs_growfs /"`
6. Verify `podman machine ssh -- "df -h /"`

-------
  - qemu-img resize — expands the virtual disk image
  - growpart — expands the partition table to use new space
  - xfs_growfs — expands the filesystem inside the partition

Thank you, @urjitbhatia! I ran into this issue on podman desktop on my Mac. Your steps solved it for me. From the UX perspective, if I change the partition size in the Desktop GUI, it should be able to reflect that change in the VM's disk. Interesting that it doesn't do so.

Thank you @urjitbhatia, your steps solved my issue!

It's definitely not great that the UI promises to resize the disk for you but still requires undocumented manual steps.
